title: Multisig mk1 chunk_set_id is path-independent (xpub fingerprint) → collides for same-xpub-different-path cosigners, breaking verify_bundle grouping
claim: For n>=2, each cosigner mk1 card's chunk_set_id is derived from the cosigner xpub fingerprint (path-independent), not the policy stub. Two slots sharing the same xpub at different derivation paths pass BIP-388 distinctness (keyed on (xpub.to_string(), path)) yet get IDENTICAL csis; verify_bundle groups supplied mk1 by csi and decodes each group as one card, so the two cards merge into one un-decodable group and both cosigners fail to map.
evidence: synthesize.rs:277 `let fp_bytes: [u8; 4] = c.xpub.fingerprint().to_bytes();` then :278 `let csi = derive_mk1_chunk_set_id(&fp_bytes);` (n>=2 branch; contrast :260 n=1 uses &stub). check_resolved_slots_distinctness (cmd/bundle.rs:446-460) rejects only when BOTH xpub.to_string() AND path are equal (lines 449-451) → same-xpub-different-path passes. verify_bundle.rs:1568-1576 groups supplied.mk1 into a BTreeMap<u32,Vec<&str>> keyed by chunk_set_id_extract; :1581-1602 calls mk_codec::decode(g) per group. mk-codec string_layer/chunk.rs:131-136 `if chunks.len() != total_usize { return Err(Error::ChunkedHeaderMalformed(...)) }` — a merged 2*T-chunk group fails the count check (duplicate chunk_index at :163-167 is also reachable). mk1 cards are ALWAYS chunked (mk-codec pipeline.rs:351-354: v0.1 encoders never emit SingleString, smallest bytecode 80B > 56B single-string cap) so every multisig mk1 carries a csi and the grouping path is always taken. The test corpus header at tests/cli_verify_bundle_multi_cosigner_mk1.rs:1-7 documents the fingerprint-based csi was the 'F1 fix' for a PRIOR all-cosigners-share-stub collision — it fixes distinct-fingerprint cosigners but re-introduces the collision for same-xpub-different-path. No test cell exercises that case (all use distinct seeds → distinct fingerprints).
suggested_fix: Make the multisig mk1 csi slot-unique and reproducible (e.g. derive from policy stub + slot index, or fold the cosigner origin path into the seed alongside the fingerprint). Add a regression test: a 2-of-2 bundle reusing one xpub at two different paths, round-tripped through verify-bundle, must map both cosigners.
verification_note: Re-grounded against current source. Confirmed synthesize.rs:277-278 fingerprint-seeded csi; confirmed distinctness keys on (xpub.to_string(), path) at cmd/bundle.rs:449-451; confirmed grouping/decode at the ACTUAL file cmd/verify_bundle.rs:1568-1602 (finding's cited path src/verify_bundle.rs is wrong — file is under src/cmd/); confirmed mk-codec chunk.rs:131-136 count-check returns ChunkedHeaderMalformed on a merged group; confirmed always-chunked via pipeline.rs:351-354. CORRECTION to the finding's user-visible verdict: in the primary mapper emit_multisig_checks, a single failed (merged) group is a DecodeFailed group → the second pass (verify_bundle.rs:1663-1672) promotes slot[0] to DecodeFailed and slot[1] stays NotSupplied; it is NOT 'both silently NotSupplied' as stated. Substance holds: two valid supplied cards yield a spurious verify failure. Kept at important — real contract violation in a funds-handling verify path; not funds loss / not wrong card bytes (emitted cards are individually correct and decodable), and the trigger config (same xpub at two paths in one multisig) is uncommon, so not critical. The 20-bit distinct-fingerprint birthday collision the finding also notes is real but astronomically rare for a normal small multisig (~k/2^20).

title: self_check_bundle does not verify mk1 card xpub <-> descriptor pubkey binding (wrong-card-data regression class uncaught)
claim: self_check_bundle validates md1 reassembly, the shared 4-byte policy_id_stub linkage, mk1 fingerprint presence/absence, and per-slot ms1 entropy round-trip — but never checks that each decoded mk1 card's xpub appears in the descriptor's pubkey set. A synthesis regression emitting a wrong/swapped xpub on an mk1 card (with the correct shared stub) would still pass --self-check.
evidence: bundle.rs:2112-2138 (Multi) decodes each card and only checks policy_id_stubs equality across cards (:2124-2131) + inclusion of expected_stub (:2132-2137); :2086-2110 (Single) checks only stub + fingerprint. No xpub_to_65(&card.xpub) comparison against desc.tlv.pubkeys, even though desc is decoded at :2067 and carries tlv.pubkeys. Contrast verify_bundle.rs:1636-1657 which DOES bind (xpub_to_65 vs pubkeys, promoting to MappingFailure::XpubNotInPolicy at :1654) — so the binding is enforced in the user-invoked verify command but NOT in self-check. The test corpus header (cli_verify_bundle_multi_cosigner_mk1.rs:12-13) independently notes '--self-check bypasses csi-grouping and passes pre-fix'.
suggested_fix: In self_check_bundle's Multi and Single branches, after decoding each card compute xpub_to_65(&card.xpub) and assert it is present in desc.tlv.pubkeys; return BundleMismatch otherwise. Cheap, closes the wrong-xpub-on-card class inside self-check.
verification_note: Confirmed at cmd/bundle.rs:2060-2139 that neither the Single nor Multi branch compares card.xpub to desc.tlv.pubkeys; desc and its tlv.pubkeys are available (decoded at :2067). Confirmed verify_bundle.rs DOES enforce the binding (XpubNotInPolicy at :1654). Minor is correct: defense-in-depth gap, not a live bug — emitted xpubs come from resolved slots by construction, and verify-bundle covers the binding for the user-facing path.

title: mk1 chunk_set_id is derived from two different sources for n=1 (policy stub) vs n>=2 (xpub fingerprint), and the displayed engraving-card mk1_card_id mismatches the embedded csi for multisig
claim: n=1 seeds the mk1 csi from the policy stub (derive_mk1_chunk_set_id(&stub)) while n>=2 seeds it from the xpub fingerprint. The engraving-card display helper computes mk1_card_id from the policy stub for ALL slots, so for multisig the human-readable card id printed does NOT match the actual csi embedded in the emitted mk1 strings. csi is non-load-bearing for decode, so not a funds bug, but the divergent derivations plus display/encode mismatch are confusing and are the root of mk1-csi-collision-multisig-grouping.
evidence: synthesize.rs:260 `let csi = derive_mk1_chunk_set_id(&stub);` (n==1) vs :278 `let csi = derive_mk1_chunk_set_id(&fp_bytes);` (n>=2). cmd/bundle.rs:1089-1100 always derives the displayed stub_csi_4hex from the policy stub; :1109 assigns it to mk1_card_id unconditionally; the comment at :1087-1088 ('Both ms1 and mk1 share the policy_id_stub-derived chunk_set_id') is FALSE for n>=2 mk1. mk-codec pipeline.rs:39 ('the chunk_set_id is opaque') and the test comment near pipeline.rs:228 ('the decoder doesn't care about the value, only that it's consistent across chunks') confirm csi value is not load-bearing for single-card decode.
suggested_fix: Unify both paths to one deterministic csi derivation (policy-stub + slot-index) so the encoded mk1 csi matches the displayed engraving-card id and the n=1 / n>=2 logic is identical; correct the misleading comment at cmd/bundle.rs:1087-1088. Folds naturally into the fix for mk1-csi-collision-multisig-grouping.
verification_note: Confirmed the two derivation sites (synthesize.rs:260 stub vs :278 fingerprint) and that cmd/bundle.rs:1095-1109 displays a stub-derived mk1_card_id for every slot while multisig mk1 strings embed a fingerprint-derived csi → genuine display-vs-wire mismatch and a false in-code comment. Confirmed csi non-load-bearing via mk-codec pipeline.rs:39 + the test comment. Observation is appropriate: cosmetic/consistency drift with no functional/funds impact.

title: Documented 'leading 16 bits of chunk_set_id agree across md1/mk1/ms1' anti-collision invariant is not actually true
location: crates/mnemonic-toolkit/CHANGELOG.md:1899 (technical-manual §IV.2); md-codec chunk.rs:242-244; toolkit synthesize.rs:260,278; ms-codec encode.rs:16
claim: The manual documents that the leading 16 bits of chunk_set_id agree across all three cards from one bundle. In reality md1's csi = derive_chunk_set_id(compute_md1_encoding_id(d)) (the ENCODING id), mk1's = derive_mk1_chunk_set_id(policy_id[..4]) for n=1 / xpub fingerprint for n>=2 (the POLICY id, a different SHA-256 preimage), and ms1 is a single string with no csi at all. Distinct hash preimages → no shared-16-bit-prefix property; nothing enforces or relies on it. Manual/intent-vs-reality drift, not a funds bug.
evidence: md-codec chunk.rs:242-244 `let md1_id = compute_md1_encoding_id(d)?; let chunk_set_id = derive_chunk_set_id(&md1_id);`. md-codec identity.rs:39-44 compute_md1_encoding_id hashes the canonical bytes; identity.rs:172-239 compute_wallet_policy_id hashes a DIFFERENT hash_input (:236) — two separate SHA-256 constructions. toolkit synthesize.rs:260 seeds mk1 csi from the policy stub. ms-codec encode.rs:16 produces a single string and `grep -rn chunk_set_id ms-codec/src` returns nothing → ms1 has no chunk_set_id. The CHANGELOG text at :1899 reads 'leading 16 bits agree across all three cards from one bundle'.
suggested_fix: Correct technical-manual §IV.2 (and CHANGELOG:1899) to describe what is actually shared (the 4-byte policy_id_stub embedded in the mk1 KeyCard payload, checked by self_check_bundle and verify_bundle) rather than a chunk_set_id 16-bit prefix the code does not produce. No code change required for funds safety.
verification_note: Confirmed all three csis derive from independent sources: md1 from compute_md1_encoding_id (chunk.rs:242-244, identity.rs:39-44), mk1 from compute_wallet_policy_id stub (synthesize.rs:260) — distinct preimage from identity.rs:172-239 — and ms1 has no chunk_set_id at all (ms-codec grep empty; encode.rs:16 single string). The documented 16-bit-prefix-agreement invariant is therefore false and unenforced. Observation is correct: doc/code drift, no funds impact.

title: BIP-388 wallet-policy round-trip discards the policy name (hardcoded 'imported-descriptor' on emit; name read-then-dropped on expand)
claim: descriptor_to_bip388_wallet_policy hardcodes the policy name on emit and expand_bip388_policy deserializes the policy name into _name but never threads it through, so a --format bip388 round-trip loses any caller-meaningful name. Descriptor body and keys are byte-faithful; NOT funds loss.
evidence: Confirmed at pipeline.rs:207 the emit literal is `"name": "imported-descriptor"`, hardcoded regardless of input. Confirmed at wallet_import/pipeline.rs:161-164 the field is `#[serde(rename = "name")] _name: String` (comment explicitly: 'Deserialized-but-unread'). Confirmed expand_bip388_policy (lines 187-207) returns only the expanded descriptor STRING (Ok(template)) and never reads parsed._name — only description_template and keys_info are used (193-200).
suggested_fix: If a stable name is desired, thread the policy name through expand_bip388_policy (return it alongside the descriptor) so emitters with a name slot can reuse it; otherwise document the lossiness explicitly in --help / manual. No funds impact.
verification_note: Independently re-grounded at both cited sites. Emit truly hardcodes 'imported-descriptor' (pipeline.rs:207). On import, BipPolicyJson reads name into _name (rename="name" is load-bearing under deny_unknown_fields) and expand_bip388_policy's return is Ok(template) — _name is never consulted. Round-trip name-loss is real; descriptor/keys are faithful, so no funds impact. Severity observation upheld.

title: gate localize() collapses ALL from_str_ext(insane) errors to None, vs localize_parse_failure which narrows to NonTopLevel
claim: localize() maps every Err(_) from per-subtree from_str_ext(insane) to None, relying on the M3 invariant that after step-2 type-check the only subtree failure can be NonTopLevel; localize_parse_failure correctly narrows to Error::NonTopLevel. If step-2's type-check is ever loosened, localize() would silently mis-point a step-3 sanity diagnostic to 'root' instead of failing/narrowing. Diagnostic-precision robustness only — the gate still fails closed (policy still refused; only the node_path label could be wrong).
evidence: Confirmed gate.rs:452 `Err(_) => None,` under the multi-line INVARIANT(M3) comment (446-451) which itself states 'If step 2 were ever relaxed, this collapse would need to re-narrow to Error::NonTopLevel.' Confirmed the parallel localize_parse_failure narrows: gate.rs:415 `Err(miniscript::Error::NonTopLevel(_)) => None,` then gate.rs:416 `Err(_) => Some(path.to_string())`. Confirmed fail-closed: step-3 sanity failures `return Err(vec![localize_sanity(...)])` (gate.rs:188); localize's result only sets the diagnostic `path` label via `.unwrap_or_else(|| "root")` (gate.rs:353).
suggested_fix: Narrow localize()'s catch to `Err(miniscript::Error::NonTopLevel(_)) => None` and treat other errors as a defensive root fallback (or debug_assert), so a future step-2 relaxation surfaces rather than silently mislabels.
verification_note: Independently confirmed the asymmetry between localize (gate.rs:452 swallow-all) and localize_parse_failure (gate.rs:415-416 narrow). Traced step ordering: step-2 from_str failure returns early (gate.rs:171-173), step-3 sanity failure returns Err with the localized diagnostic (gate.rs:188); localize() only computes the node-path LABEL (gate.rs:353, falls back to 'root'). So the policy is refused regardless of localize's behavior — this is a diagnostic-label precision issue, not a fail-open. Severity minor upheld.

title: gate step-1 timelock field check rejects only older>=2^31 / after==0; full BIP-65/68 range validity is deferred to step-2 from_str
claim: validate_fields rejects older(N) only when N==0 or N>=2^31 and after(N) only when N==0; other out-of-range/semantically-odd timelocks are left to step-2 from_str. Fail-closed: an invalid timelock is still refused, just with a TypeError diagnostic localized to the node rather than a friendlier SchemaField message. Noting so future readers don't assume step-1 is a complete timelock validator.
evidence: Confirmed gate.rs:244-251 Older check `if *n == 0 || *n >= (1u32 << 31)` (rejects 0 and the CSV disable bit only). Confirmed gate.rs:252-256 After check `if *n == 0` (rejects 0 only; no BIP-65 height/time boundary check). Step-2 from_str (gate.rs:171) is the authoritative parse and rejects ranges miniscript's AbsLockTime/RelLockTime won't accept (to_miniscript uses AbsLockTime::from_consensus / RelLockTime::from_consensus which error on invalid range).
suggested_fix: Optional: extend the after() field check with the BIP-65 height/time boundary for a friendlier SchemaField diagnostic, or add a doc note that step-2 from_str is the authoritative timelock-range gate. No funds impact.
verification_note: Confirmed both checks at gate.rs:245 and gate.rs:253 are exactly as described — step-1 is intentionally a partial validator. Verified fail-closed routing: any value that escapes step-1 reaches step-2 from_str which is the type/range oracle and returns early on failure (gate.rs:171-173). No silent-accept path exists for an out-of-range timelock. Severity observation upheld.

title: inspect/repair accept secret ms1 card on argv without the secret_in_argv_warning advisory every other command emits
claim: Both `inspect --ms1 ms1abc...` and `repair --ms1 ms1abc...` place a secret-bearing ms1 card (BIP-39 entropy; OutputClass::PrivateKeyMaterial) on argv without ever calling crate::secret_advisory::secret_in_argv_warning, unlike 14+ other commands that emit the advisory for inline secrets. Both support the `--ms1 -` stdin alternative, so a concrete pointer exists and a clear precedent for firing.
evidence: Verified at source: inspect.rs run() (lines 88-156) only emits emit_output_class_advisory at :152; repair.rs run() (101-218) only emit_output_class_advisory at :208 — neither references secret_in_argv_warning anywhere in the file. secret_advisory.rs::card_kind_class (:90) maps CardKind::Ms1 -> OutputClass::PrivateKeyMaterial. repair.rs::resolve_groups (:262-342) implements the `-` stdin dash-expansion route (:325-341), and inspect.rs:33 documents `--ms1 -`. By contrast secret_in_argv_warning is called inline-for-secrets across addresses/bundle/convert/derive_child/electrum_decrypt/final_word/import_wallet/ms_shares/nostr/restore/seedqr/seed_xor/silent_payment/slip39/xpub_search. design/FOLLOWUPS.md has no exemption entry for inspect/repair argv advisory.
suggested_fix: In inspect::run and repair::run, before decoding, iterate the resolved ms1 inputs and for any inline (non-`-`) ms1 value call crate::secret_advisory::secret_in_argv_warning(stderr, "--ms1", "--ms1 -"), mirroring xpub_search/seed_intake.rs:104.
verification_note: Independently re-grounded: confirmed zero secret_in_argv_warning references in both inspect.rs and repair.rs, confirmed ms1 IS PrivateKeyMaterial via card_kind_class at secret_advisory.rs:90, confirmed the `--ms1 -` stdin route is real (resolve_groups dash-expansion repair.rs:325-341), and confirmed no FOLLOWUPS exemption. Severity left at minor: real exposure is mitigated by main.rs set_non_dumpable (blocks cross-UID /proc/cmdline) and the secret never reaches stdout/logs — advisory-coverage UX gap, not an actual leak.

title: Emitted private-key hex/WIF strings in silent-payment and nostr are plain String, not Zeroizing
claim: scan_priv/spend_priv (silent_payment.rs:256-257 = hex::encode of the derived scan/spend secret bytes) and the nostr WIF (nostr.rs:212 wif_for) are plain String values carried into JSON envelope structs without zeroize-on-drop, lingering unscrubbed in heap until the allocator reuses the pages — while the upstream input secrets ARE wrapped (Zeroizing + mlock).
evidence: Verified at source: silent_payment.rs:256-257 `let scan_priv = hex::encode(b_scan.secret_bytes());` / `let spend_priv = hex::encode(b_spend.secret_bytes());` (both String), moved into SilentPaymentJson at :271-272 whose fields scan_priv/spend_priv are `String` (decl :97-98). nostr.rs:128 `pub fn wif_for(...) -> String`; the result is bound at :212 and used at :221 (`format!("{p}{wif}")` electrum field — an additional unscrubbed copy), :231 (`wif: Some(wif.clone())`), and :249 (text print). No Zeroizing wrapper on any of these derived serializations.
suggested_fix: Wrap the derived hex/WIF strings (and the electrum-prefixed copy) in zeroize::Zeroizing so they scrub on drop, matching the Zeroizing<String> discipline already used for inputs in these same handlers.
verification_note: Independently confirmed scan_priv/spend_priv are plain String from hex::encode and land in String struct fields (silent_payment.rs:97-98,256-257,271-272), and that wif_for returns plain String (nostr.rs:128) consumed unscrubbed at :212/:221/:231/:249. Noted an extra unscrubbed WIF copy at nostr.rs:221 the original finding only mentioned in passing. Severity left at minor: these are the command's intended stdout output (small window) and the residual is consistent with the accepted upstream-zeroize posture of rust-secp256k1/rust-bitcoin; not a leak to an unintended sink.

title: silent-payment --secret phrase path hardcodes BIP-39 English; only the ms1 path honors wire language
claim: resolve_master_xpriv parses a raw BIP-39 phrase with bip39::Language::English unconditionally (line 155), while the ms1 branch (lines 138-141) correctly resolves per-card wire language via payload_bip39_language. silent-payment has no --language flag, so a non-English raw phrase cannot be used — a capability limitation, not funds loss (fail-safe: a non-English phrase fails checksum parse, not mis-derives).
evidence: Verified at source: silent_payment.rs:155 `bip39::Mnemonic::parse_in(bip39::Language::English, s)`; contrast :138-141 `crate::language::payload_bip39_language(&payload, crate::language::CliLanguage::English)` for the ms1 branch. SilentPaymentArgs (lines 23-71) has no language field — only secret/secret_file/secret_stdin/passphrase/passphrase_stdin/network/account/label/change_address/json.
suggested_fix: Either add a --language flag threaded into the phrase parse, or document that --secret phrase= is English-only (the ms1 route covers other languages via the card's wire language).
verification_note: Independently confirmed line 155 hardcodes English for the raw-phrase branch, the ms1 branch honors wire language at :138-141, and SilentPaymentArgs has no --language flag. Confirmed the behavior is fail-safe: parse_in validates the wordlist+checksum, so a non-English phrase errors out (ToolkitError::SilentPayment) rather than deriving a wrong-but-valid seed — no silent wrong output. Observation severity correct.

title: addresses --from resolves @env: for non-secret xpub source, contrary to the documented secret-flag-surfaces-only sentinel rule
claim: In addresses::run the --from value is passed through resolve_env_var_sentinel unconditionally (line 162) regardless of from.node, so `addresses --from xpub=@env:VAR` (a non-secret watch-only source) also resolves the env var — contrary to the env_sentinel module doc which locks @env: resolution to the 6 secret-flag surfaces and treats non-secret flags' @env: as literal. No secret leak and no funds risk (xpub is public and cannot legitimately begin with @env:).
evidence: Verified at source: addresses.rs:159-163 resolves `crate::env_sentinel::resolve_env_var_sentinel(&from.value, "--from")` unconditionally for the non-stdin path with no is_secret_bearing gate. Contrast: bundle.rs:2255 gates the slot resolution on `s.subkey.is_secret_bearing()` and derive_child.rs:404 gates `--from` resolution on `owned.from.node.is_secret_bearing()`. convert.rs::is_secret_bearing (:94-106) EXCLUDES Xpub. env_sentinel.rs:11-13 lists `--from <node>=` secret-bearing composite nodes as phrase/entropy/wif/xprv/minikey/electrum-phrase (NOT xpub), and :18-20/:56 doc states resolution applies ONLY at the 6 secret-flag surfaces. addresses accepts xpub as a --from source (addresses.rs:166-192).
suggested_fix: Gate the addresses --from env-sentinel resolution on from.node.is_secret_bearing() (matching bundle/derive_child), or update the env_sentinel module doc to acknowledge addresses resolves @env: for all --from sources.
verification_note: Independently confirmed the unconditional resolution at addresses.rs:162, the gated resolution at the two sibling sites (bundle.rs:2255, derive_child.rs:404), that is_secret_bearing excludes Xpub (convert.rs:94-106), and the env_sentinel module doc's secret-surface-only rule. Confirmed this is a behavioral/spec-doc divergence, not a security issue: xpub is watch-only and a real xpub value (xpub/tpub/...) cannot collide with the `@env:` prefix, so the only consequence is a convenience expansion or a confusing 'env-var unset' error. Observation severity correct.

title: Secret-equivalent positionals (codex32 shares, ms1) are cloned to state.json with zero redaction
claim: redact_for_persistence() copies positionals verbatim with no filter; the three redaction drop-classes (SECRET_FLAG_NAMES, schema_secret_flag_names, SECRET_NODE_TYPES, SECRET_SLOT_SUBKEYS) match no positional. Secret-equivalent positionals (ms combine <shares>, bare ms1 on ms inspect/decode/verify/derive) would persist in plaintext when persistence is wired; they also render unmasked and never trigger run-confirm.
evidence: persistence.rs:115 `positionals: state.positionals.clone(),` with no filter. mod.rs:53-63 PositionalArgSchema struct has NO secret field. secrets.rs:200-225 should_confirm_run iterates subcommand.flags, state.slots.rows, state.values — never state.positionals. main.rs:664-675 renders positionals via plain ui.text_edit_singleline(&mut state.positionals[i]). ms.rs:442-447 COMBINE_POSITIONALS help: 'Distributed share strings to recombine (K or more, distinct indices). Secret-equivalent.'
suggested_fix: Add a secret: bool field to PositionalArgSchema, add a redaction arm in redact_for_persistence dropping secret positionals, route them through SecretLineEdit, and extend should_confirm_run to check positionals. Until then persistence MUST NOT be wired (it currently is not).
verification_note: Independently confirmed at source: persistence.rs:115 clones positionals with zero filter; PositionalArgSchema (mod.rs:53-63) has no secret field so positionals cannot be classified secret; should_confirm_run (secrets.rs:205-223) inspects flags+slots+NodeValueComposite only, never state.positionals; positionals render via bare text_edit_singleline at main.rs:675; ms.rs:445 help literally says 'Secret-equivalent.' This is a LATENT leak (persistence save/load currently have zero callers in src/), so it cannot leak today but becomes live the instant Phase 8 wires save(). Severity kept important: FOLLOWUP positional-secrets-not-redacted-at-persist is filed and open (FOLLOWUPS.md:68), and the redaction layer's drop set provably excludes positionals.

title: blank_xprv_keys only catches xprv-prefix — a WIF or raw-hex private key in a tree key/keys field persists unredacted
claim: is_xprv_like matches only the extended-private prefix shape (prv at byte 1..4 after stripping [origin]). blank_xprv_keys blanks only those. The toolkit gate documents WIF/raw-hex secrets as not prefix-detectable. redacted_for_persistence runs unconditionally (no validate gate), so a WIF (K/L/c-prefix base58) or raw-hex private key pasted into a key/keys row survives redaction and would be written to state.json verbatim. Distinct from the resolved hex/w-field FOLLOWUP.
evidence: tree_model.rs:650-653 is_xprv_like checks only key_part.as_bytes().get(1..4) == Some(b"prv"). tree_model.rs:657-669 blank_xprv_keys uses only is_xprv_like. tree_model.rs:176-187 redacted_for_persistence calls blank_xprv_keys unconditionally — no validate_ok branch. gate.rs:275-276 (toolkit) verbatim: 'WIF / raw-hex secrets are not prefix-detectable here; they are refused by the step-2 from_str type-check'.
suggested_fix: Do not rely on a prefix heuristic the toolkit itself documents as incomplete. Either blank all key/keys content unless it positively matches an xpub/descriptor-pubkey shape (allowlist), or refuse to persist a tree that has not passed the watch-only gate. File a FOLLOWUP covering WIF/hex-private-key in key/keys fields.
verification_note: Confirmed at source: is_xprv_like (tree_model.rs:650-653) only detects extended-private prefixes; the toolkit's own check_secret_key doc-comment at gate.rs:275-276 states verbatim 'WIF / raw-hex secrets are not prefix-detectable here; they are refused by the step-2 from_str type-check' — so a WIF/hex priv key passes the GUI's redaction walk untouched. redacted_for_persistence (tree_model.rs:176-178) calls blank_xprv_keys unconditionally with no validate_ok gate (validate_ok is merely nulled at :184). Genuinely distinct from the RESOLVED FOLLOWUP tree-xprv-heuristic-only-covers-key-fields (FOLLOWUPS.md:26) which covered xprv in hex/w fields, not non-xprv private keys in key/keys fields. LATENT (persistence unwired). Severity kept important: a real gap the toolkit explicitly flagged as undetectable, with no FOLLOWUP tracking this specific case.

title: Paste-warn modal is documented as a live mitigation but is never wired — should_warn_on_paste/PASTE_WARN_MODAL_TEXT are dead code
claim: PASTE_WARN_MODAL_TEXT and should_warn_on_paste are never called anywhere in src/. SecretLineEdit does no paste detection. The paste-warning affordance described in module prose and SPEC does not exist in the running app; a user pasting a seed phrase gets no warning about argv/process-table/shell-history exposure despite the code presenting it as shipped.
evidence: grep should_warn_on_paste|PASTE_WARN_MODAL_TEXT across src/ returns ONLY the definitions in secrets.rs (lines 167, 194-195) plus PASTE_WARN_THRESHOLD used only inside should_warn_on_paste itself. main.rs wires should_confirm_run (749) + RUN_CONFIRM_MODAL_PREFIX (839) but no paste path. secret_widget.rs::show does no paste detection.
suggested_fix: Either wire the paste-warn modal into SecretLineEdit paste handling (show once per session on a >= PASTE_WARN_THRESHOLD paste into a secret-class widget) or file an explicit FOLLOWUP and downgrade the module prose so PASTE_WARN_MODAL_TEXT is not presented as an active mitigation.

title: The command Preview line and run-confirm modal display secret argv tokens in cleartext

title: mnemonic-gui release tags run NO tests — every drift gate + pin-coherence check is bypassed on the release commit
claim: On a mnemonic-gui-v* tag push only build.yml fires (clippy + cargo build --release + artifact upload + GitHub release) — it runs NO cargo test. The whole test suite (schema_mirror, archetype_schema_mirror, spec_nodes_mirror, pin_coherence, readme_pin_coherence, schema_mirror_secret_drift, secret_taxonomy_pin) lives in schema-mirror.yml's cargo test steps, and schema-mirror.yml has no tags: trigger. So release artifacts can be built/published from a commit whose drift/pin-coherence gates never ran (re-tag, hotfix-branch tag, force-push reconciliation, tag cut before the master-push run finishes).
evidence: build.yml has only clippy/build/release jobs; no cargo test line anywhere in the file. Its tag-gated jobs (upload-artifact :110-115 if startsWith ...refs/tags/mnemonic-gui-v, release :117-132) publish via download-artifact + softprops/action-gh-release. schema-mirror.yml:111 cargo test --test schema_mirror and :127 cargo test --workspace are the ONLY two test invocations in the entire gui workflow dir, and schema-mirror.yml:3-7 triggers on push branches:[master] / pull_request only — no tags. pin_coherence.rs and readme_pin_coherence.rs are pure-logic gates whose whole job is to validate the release's own version/pin markers, and they run only inside cargo test --workspace (schema-mirror.yml:127).
suggested_fix: Add tags: ['mnemonic-gui-v*'] to schema-mirror.yml's push trigger (or add a cargo test --workspace step to build.yml's tag path) so drift + pin-coherence gates are a hard precondition of cutting a release, matching the toolkit's tag-gated install-pin-check.yml (:27-30 on push tags mnemonic-toolkit-v*) and changelog-check.yml (:17-20).
verification_note: Confirmed at source: build.yml triggers on tags (:6) but contains zero cargo test; clippy job runs only cargo clippy (:30), build job only cargo build/cross build (:86,:90). schema-mirror.yml on: block (:3-7) has push branches:[master] + pull_request only, NO tags:, and its :111/:127 are the only cargo test lines in either workflow. Independently confirmed the toolkit DOES tag-gate (install-pin-check.yml:27-30 and changelog-check.yml:17-20 both on push tags mnemonic-toolkit-v*). Also confirmed pin_coherence.rs and readme_pin_coherence.rs are the version/pin-marker gates and live only in cargo test --workspace. Severity important upheld: real release-gating gap, correctly scoped by the auditor to re-tag/hotfix/force-push cases (the normal tag==master-HEAD flow happens to have transited a master-push run).

title: toolkit g6 cross-repo mlock.rs byte-equality invariant checks sibling at moving master, not the pinned ms-cli tag
claim: The g6-invariant job asserts byte-equality of toolkit's mlock.rs against mnemonic-secret's mlock.rs but checks out the sibling at ref: master rather than the pinned ms-cli-v0.7.0 tag the constellation ships. title: flag_is_secret is a hand-maintained name allowlist with no completeness gate; a new secret VALUE flag absent from it would emit secret:false (documented accepted boundary)
claim: The leak-prevention chain anchors on secrets::flag_is_secret(name), a hardcoded matches! allowlist of ~11 flag names. gui_schema.rs:1196 derives the schema's per-flag secret bit from exactly this predicate, and the GUI's schema_mirror_secret_drift compares the GUI hand-code against that same schema bit — both projections of flag_is_secret, so they agree by construction and cannot catch a name MISSING from flag_is_secret. There is no gate over flag_is_secret's own completeness. A new secret-bearing flag named outside the list and not routed via --from/--slot would emit secret:false -> GUI persists plaintext -> all three gates stay green. The lint_argv_secret_flags module doc explicitly acknowledges this and argues such a flag would also escape runtime advisory/zeroize/GUI-mask (a more visible defect). Observation: real but documented, surfaces via more visible defenses.
evidence: secrets.rs:49-64 pub fn flag_is_secret with matches! over 11 names (--passphrase, --passphrase-stdin, --bip38-passphrase, --bip38-passphrase-stdin, --decrypt-password, --decrypt-password-stdin, --digits, --ms1, --secret, --secret-stdin, --share). gui_schema.rs:1196 let secret = mnemonic_toolkit::secrets::flag_is_secret(&name). schema_mirror_secret_drift.rs:1-26 module doc confirms it collects {(sub,flag)|flag.secret} from the gui-schema output (the flag_is_secret projection) and set-compares to the GUI hand-code. lint_argv_secret_flags.rs:32-36 module doc: 'A future secret VALUE flag added with a name absent from flag_is_secret AND not routed via --from/--slot would escape this closure — but it would also escape the runtime advisory/zeroize/GUI-mask (a far more visible defect) ... There is no separate gate over flag_is_secret completeness itself.'
suggested_fix: Optional hardening: a toolkit-side review-checklist item (or heuristic lint) that any new flag whose name contains passphrase/password/secret/share/seed/key/digits/wif/xprv/entropy must be in flag_is_secret or explicitly annotated non-secret, backstopping the by-design boundary. No code change forced.
verification_note: Confirmed verbatim: secrets.rs:49-64 is the 11-name matches! allowlist; gui_schema.rs:1196 derives the schema secret bit straight from flag_is_secret; schema_mirror_secret_drift.rs:8-13 module doc states the schema secret field 'is its projection' of flag_is_secret and the GUI hand-code 'must mirror this projection' — so both sides derive from flag_is_secret and agree by construction on a missing name (gate blind to completeness). lint_argv_secret_flags.rs:32-36 doc matches the quoted text exactly. Observation severity correct: the hole is structurally real (no completeness gate) but explicitly documented-accepted and would surface through the runtime advisory/zeroize/mask path; not a latent silent bug in any currently-shipped flag (all current secret flags are in the list).

title: import-json envelope declares schema_version but no consumer ever validates it
claim: The import-json envelope carries schema_version (outer "1", inner bundle "4") into deserialized String fields, but no consumer reads or version-gates it. serde drops unknown fields by default, so a future envelope at a bumped schema_version with renamed/repurposed fields would be silently accepted and partially mis-reconstructed by an older toolkit rather than rejected with an unsupported-version error.
evidence: json_envelope.rs:64 schema_version: String (outer), :150 schema_version: String (inner BundleJsonView). I grepped every non-test schema_version reference in the toolkit src tree: the field is SET to "4" by the producer (format.rs:120, synthesize.rs) and read only by a single test assertion (json_envelope.rs:636). Both consumer entrypoints I read — export_wallet.rs:657-684 (parse_import_json_envelopes -> envelope_to_resolved_slots) and bundle.rs:1736-1757 — never inspect schema_version. No UnsupportedVersion / version-compare exists on this path. Contrast: the codebase DOES gate elsewhere (build_descriptor.rs:282 SUPPORTED_SCHEMA_VERSION; GUI tree_model.rs:484 'if version != 1 { return Err(unsupported schema_version) }').
suggested_fix: After deserializing the envelope, reject any inner/outer schema_version the build does not support (mirror the SUPPORTED_SCHEMA_VERSION pattern at build_descriptor.rs:282 or the GUI floor at tree_model.rs:484) so a forward-incompatible envelope fails loud instead of silently mis-mapping fields.
verification_note: Confirmed at source that NO consumer validates schema_version (grepped all non-test refs; both run_from_import_json paths skip it). CORRECTED the auditor's comparison citations: md-codec 'SpecDoc::parse / ir.rs:179-182 UnsupportedVersion' does NOT exist (no schema_version reject anywhere in descriptor-mnemonic src — that citation is fabricated/stale), but the GUI tree_model.rs:484 precedent IS real and the toolkit's own build_descriptor.rs:282 SUPPORTED_SCHEMA_VERSION gate is a real in-repo precedent for the same pattern. DOWNGRADED important->minor: this is a constellation-internal wire format produced and consumed by the same toolkit; the only realistic mis-map is an OLDER binary fed a NEWER envelope (forward-compat), serde-drop-unknowns makes a same-or-older envelope safe, and current consumers depend on a small stable field set — a robustness/forward-compat hardening, not a live bug on any path that exists today.

title: Toolkit HRP classifiers use case-sensitive starts_with("ms1"/"mk1"/"md1") and reject valid all-uppercase codec strings the codecs accept
claim: The toolkit's card-type prefix probes match lowercase ms1/mk1/md1 only, but the codecs decode all-uppercase strings per BIP-173 (mk-codec rejects only Mixed case then to_lowercase-normalizes). So a valid uppercase MK1.../MS1.../MD1... card is misrouted: restore.rs:1026 is false for an uppercase mk1 cosigner -> falls through to Xpub::from_str("MK1...") which errors with a confusing 'xpub parse' message instead of decoding the mk1.
evidence: repair.rs:107-112 only checks lowercase prefixes (starts_with("ms1"/"mk1"/"md1")), and its own doc-comment :103-105 states 'The prefix-match is case-sensitive on the codec convention (lowercase per BIP-93).' restore.rs:1026 'values.iter().all(|v| v.starts_with("mk1"))'. In the ACTUAL mk-codec source (crates/mk-codec/src/string_layer/bch.rs, not the auditor's stale bch.rs:648 path): :648-649 rejects ONLY CaseStatus::Mixed, :651 'let s_lower = s.to_lowercase();', and the unit test :792 'assert_eq!(case_check("MD1QQ"), CaseStatus::Upper)' proves all-uppercase decodes fine in the codec.
suggested_fix: Case-insensitively compare (or lowercase the input) before the HRP prefix probe in classify_hrp_prefix and the ad-hoc starts_with sites, matching the codecs' BIP-173 case tolerance. Robustness only — every current path lands on a hard error, not a silent wrong result.
verification_note: Verified the codec/toolkit asymmetry directly. mk-codec case handling confirmed in the REAL file crates/mk-codec/src/string_layer/bch.rs:648-651 + test :792 (the auditor's cited path 'mnemonic-key bch.rs:648' was the old/flat layout — corrected). Toolkit case-sensitivity confirmed at repair.rs:107-112 and restore.rs:1026. Severity correctly minor: every affected path terminates on a hard error (confusing message, but no silent wrong output or funds risk).

title: build-descriptor node-grammar coverage guard is hand-list-vs-hand-list (vacuous if a PolicyNode variant is jointly omitted), unlike the proven secret-taxonomy macro
claim: node_kinds_cover_enum only compares all_variant_samples() against NODE_KINDS -- both hand-lists -- so a new PolicyNode variant added to the enum but forgotten in all_variant_samples() passes the test vacuously. The macro pattern that DOES force compile-error coverage already exists for the secret taxonomy, so the weaker guard here is an inconsistency, not a missing technique.
evidence: ir.rs:346-347 builds 'produced' from all_variant_samples() and 'expected' from NODE_KINDS -- neither is enum-exhaustive. The schema.rs:104-118 comment explicitly concedes: 'Neither catches a variant jointly omitted from all three (enum->list is author discipline; an airtight guard would need a variant-enumerator macro).' The exact macro it describes exists at convert.rs:1767 'macro_rules! declare_node_type_variants!' (used :1781).
suggested_fix: Apply the existing declare_*_variants! macro pattern to PolicyNode so a new variant produces a non-exhaustive-match compile error, forcing simultaneous updates to NODE_KINDS + NODE_GRAMMAR.
verification_note: Confirmed verbatim at source: ir.rs:345-354 node_kinds_cover_enum compares all_variant_samples().kind() set against NODE_KINDS set (both hand-maintained, neither exhaustive over the enum); the schema.rs:104-118 source comment concedes the joint-omission gap in its own words; and the declare_node_type_variants! compile-forcing macro precedent exists at convert.rs:1767. Severity correctly minor: bounded consequence -- a forgotten variant is just not surfaced as a buildable node-kind; no wrong output, no funds risk.

title: miniscript [patch.crates-io] unifies the dep graph; md-codec's hardcoded SortedMultiA refusal message is stale-in-context but the route-around keeps behavior safe
location: toolkit Cargo.toml:9-16 ([patch.crates-io] miniscript git rev 95fdd1c); descriptor-mnemonic crates/md-codec/src/to_miniscript.rs:406-408 (SortedMultiA refusal message)
claim: Toolkit patches miniscript to git rev 95fdd1c (has Terminal::SortedMultiA) for its entire dep graph, so md-codec is recompiled against the patched miniscript inside the toolkit binary; md-codec's to_miniscript.rs unconditionally refuses SortedMultiA with the message 'rust-miniscript v13 has no Terminal::SortedMultiA fragment' -- factually wrong inside the patched build, but the refusal still fires because md-codec hand-builds Terminal fragments. The toolkit routes around this (v0.49.1 derive on the descriptor STRING). Net: safe (refuses rather than mis-renders), but the divergence is load-bearing and the misleading message could mislead a future maintainer.
evidence: toolkit Cargo.toml:9-16 [patch.crates-io] miniscript = git rev '95fdd1c5773bd918c574d2225787973f63e16a66' with comment citing PR #910 (sortedmulti_a parser) + #915. descriptor-mnemonic to_miniscript.rs:406-408 hardcodes the arm '(Tag::SortedMultiA, Body::MultiKeys { .. }) => ...' with message '...rust-miniscript v13 has no Terminal::SortedMultiA fragment'. md-codec also fully supports SortedMultiA at the tag/tree layers (tag.rs:35/109/173, tree.rs:226/426-430) so the refusal is purely in the manual-lowering arm, not an absolute gap.
suggested_fix: When the miniscript crates.io release containing #910+#915 publishes (FOLLOWUP tr-sortedmulti-a-via-upstream / v0.3.2 cleanup), drop the patch in lockstep. Separately update md-codec to_miniscript.rs:408's message to say the refusal is a md-codec manual-lowering limitation (or capability-detect) so it doesn't mislead in patched builds.
verification_note: Confirmed the patch pin (Cargo.toml:9-16, rev 95fdd1c, comment references PR #910+#915 and the v0.3.2-drop FOLLOWUP) and the hardcoded stale message (to_miniscript.rs:406-408). Confirmed md-codec supports SortedMultiA everywhere except this manual-lowering arm (tag.rs/tree.rs). Correctly an observation: behavior is safe (refuse not mis-render); only the misleading error text + the load-bearing-ness of the patch are worth recording. (Did not re-verify the Cargo.lock line numbers the auditor cited; the patch+message core is independently confirmed and is what carries the finding.)

title: verify-bundle groups multisig mk1 cards by a chunk_set_id derived from the cosigner BIP-32 fingerprint, assuming distinct fingerprints; defended by mk-codec cross-chunk hash
location: crates/mnemonic-toolkit/src/synthesize.rs:260 (single-sig csi from stub), :277-280 (multisig csi from c.xpub.fingerprint().to_bytes()); cmd/verify_bundle.rs:1571-1576 (group supplied.mk1 by chunk_set_id); mnemonic-key crates/mk-codec/src/string_layer/chunk.rs:131,152,160,189-195 (reassemble integrity guards)
claim: In multisig emission each cosigner's mk1 chunk_set_id is derived from the 4-byte BIP-32 fingerprint, and verify_bundle re-groups supplied mk1 chunks by chunk_set_id to reassemble per-cosigner. Two cosigners sharing a 4-byte fingerprint (1-in-2^32, or adversarial) collide on chunk_set_id and group together -- but this is CAUGHT not mis-decoded, because mk-codec's reassemble enforces chunks.len()==total_chunks plus a trailing SHA-256[0..4] cross-chunk hash.
evidence: synthesize.rs:260 single-sig 'derive_mk1_chunk_set_id(&stub)' (policy-bound) vs :277-278 multisig 'let fp_bytes: [u8;4] = c.xpub.fingerprint().to_bytes(); let csi = derive_mk1_chunk_set_id(&fp_bytes)' (per-cosigner fingerprint). verify_bundle.rs:1572 'chunked.entry(csi).or_default().push(s.as_str())' groups purely by csi. mk-codec chunk.rs:131 'if chunks.len() != total_usize' (count guard), :152-154 total_chunks-disagree guard, :160 chunk_index-bounds guard, :189-195 cross-chunk SHA-256[0..4] hash verify.
suggested_fix: No action required for safety (the codec defends it). Optionally add a verify-bundle assertion that the number of distinct chunk_set_id groups equals the cosigner count, converting a fingerprint collision into an explicit toolkit-level diagnostic instead of a downstream CrossChunkHashMismatch.
verification_note: Confirmed the single-vs-multi csi-derivation asymmetry verbatim at synthesize.rs:260 (stub) vs :277-278 (xpub.fingerprint().to_bytes()), the verify_bundle grouping at :1571-1576, and ALL FOUR mk-codec reassemble integrity guards at chunk.rs:131/152/160/189-195 (count + total agreement + index bounds + cross-chunk hash). Correctly an observation: a fingerprint collision is caught by the codec's downstream integrity checks (no silent mis-decode), and the probability is 2^-32 accidental. Worth knowing only because the grouping key relies on the codec's guards rather than a toolkit-side distinctness guarantee.

title: secret_flag_enumeration_matches_authoritative_predicate is vacuous — compares flag_is_secret against itself; no completeness gate over secret-flag classification
claim: The test presented as the toolkit-internal half of the secret-flag drift gate cannot fail because both sides of its equality derive from the same function. It provides ZERO protection against a new secret-bearing CLI flag added without classifying it in secrets::flag_is_secret — which would silently disable GUI masking / exit-zeroize sweep treatment for that flag's value (a secret-handling-class regression).
evidence: I confirmed at source: gui_schema.rs::emit_flag line 1196 sets the emitted per-flag `secret` field via `let secret = mnemonic_toolkit::secrets::flag_is_secret(&name);`. cli_gui_schema_v5_extensions.rs:297-298 reads that same emitted field into `emitted_secret` and asserts it equals `flag_is_secret(name)` (`expected_secret`) — both operands are flag_is_secret(name), so the assert_eq at :299-304 is a tautology. flag_is_secret (secrets.rs:50-63) is a hardcoded `matches!` over 11 literal flag names; a new secret VALUE flag with a name absent from it is emitted secret:false AND predicate returns false → they agree → test passes. The would-be backstop lint also cannot catch it: lint_argv_secret_flags.rs::live_secret_flag_routes (line 155) reads the gui-schema `secret` bit, which is transitively flag_is_secret, and the module doc at lines 30-36 explicitly states 'There is no separate gate over flag_is_secret completeness itself.' The secrets.rs unit tests (:70-129) are hand-curated name lists, not a live-clap-surface enumeration. I grepped tests/ and src/ and found no clap-walk/name-heuristic backstop.
suggested_fix: Add a real completeness cell that walks the live clap/gui-schema arg surface and applies a NAME heuristic (long name containing passphrase/secret/password/share/seed/mnemonic/wif/xprv/entropy/digits and not a path/file/boolean-toggle), failing on a heuristic-positive flag that flag_is_secret returns false for. Alternatively drive the test's `expected_secret` from an independent allow-list literal kept in the test (not flag_is_secret) so the comparison is non-circular.
verification_note: Independently confirmed the tautology by reading both halves: emitted field = flag_is_secret(name) at gui_schema.rs:1196, and the test re-derives expected = flag_is_secret(name) at :298 then assert_eq's them. Also confirmed the named alternative gate (lint_argv_secret_flags) is transitive on the same predicate (live_secret_flag_routes reads the gui-schema secret bit at :155) and the module doc concedes the gap (:30-36). Kept at important not critical: runtime argv-leak warnings are hand-wired per-call-site literals (secret_in_argv_warning(stderr, "--passphrase", ...) across cmd/*.rs), so a misclassified flag's concrete impact is missing GUI-mask/zeroize-class treatment — a real secret-handling contract gap, not a demonstrated unconditional plaintext leak in the toolkit itself.

title: 25 of 26 tests/vectors/v0_2 multisig md1/mk1/ms1 goldens are read by no test (orphaned corpus; v0.48.0 cleanup only removed the 8 taproot ones)
claim: The v0_2 vector directory holds 26 golden bundle fixtures; only bip84-mainnet-0-false-true.txt is read by any test. The 25 multisig fixtures are dead goldens — a regression changing the bundle multisig md1/mk1/ms1 wire output for these shapes would be caught by no test. Same orphaned-golden false-green class v0.48.0 partially cleaned (deleted 8 tr-* orphans, left these 25).
evidence: I listed the directory: 26 .txt files. I grepped tests/ and src/ for each fixture's basename stem (excluding self-references): bip84-mainnet-0-false-true.txt = 1 reference (cli_self_check.rs:13 std::fs::read_to_string); all 25 multisig stems (wsh-multi-*, wsh-sortedmulti-*, sh-wsh-multi-*, sh-wsh-sortedmulti-*, including -5-false-false and -true-false variants) = 0 references each. I grepped tests/ and src/ for read_dir/insta::glob/WalkDir/vectors\/v0_2 and found no directory-driven harness. The deleted multisig self-check is tombstoned at cli_self_check.rs:34-36.
suggested_fix: Resolve the open FOLLOWUP: delete the 25 dead multisig vectors, or wire a read_dir-driven harness that decode-asserts each (turning them into live wire-regression pins). Do not leave them maintained-but-unread.
verification_note: Confirmed via ls (26 files) + per-basename grep over tests/ and src/: exactly 1 fixture (bip84) is read, the other 25 have 0 references, and there is no read_dir/glob harness over the directory. Confirmed this is tracked OPEN as FOLLOWUP orphaned-v0_2-md1-vectors-no-harness at design/FOLLOWUPS.md:89-95 (status: open). Minor is correct — these are dead-but-correct goldens, a test-hygiene/silent-rot risk, not a live wrong-output.

title: cli_self_check.rs module doc claims it reads a wsh-sortedmulti fixture the code never opens
claim: The module doc states fixtures live at tests/vectors/v0_2/{bip84,wsh-sortedmulti}-mainnet-0-false-true.txt, implying both are exercised. The code only reads the bip84 one. wsh-sortedmulti-mainnet-0-false-true.txt is referenced in prose but read by no code — doc-vs-code drift overstating multisig self-check coverage.
evidence: Read cli_self_check.rs:5: module doc lists the brace-expansion `{bip84,wsh-sortedmulti}-mainnet-0-false-true.txt`. The only std::fs::read_to_string in the file is at :13, reading `bip84-mainnet-0-false-true.txt`. Grep for `wsh-sortedmulti-mainnet-0-false-true` across tests/ and src/ returns nothing — the fixture exists in v0_2/ (part of the orphaned corpus above) but is opened by no code.
suggested_fix: Either add a multisig self-check cell that reads the wsh-sortedmulti fixture, or correct the module doc to list only the fixture actually read.
verification_note: Confirmed both halves: module-doc brace-list at :5 names wsh-sortedmulti, the sole read at :13 is bip84 only, and a repo-wide grep for the wsh-sortedmulti-0-false-true stem returns zero hits. Pure documentation overstatement of coverage; observation is the right severity.

title: lint_zeroize_discipline canon is a hand-frozen list with file-level substring evidence and a loose count-range smoke check — no set-equality completeness closure
claim: lint_zeroize_discipline relies on a hand-maintained ZEROIZE_ROWS list whose only completeness signal is a 17-wide count range plus per-row file-level source.contains anchors. Unlike the argv lint (which set-equals declared routes against the live gui-schema surface), this has no closure over the actual set of Zeroizing/zeroize call sites — a newly-added owned secret buffer that is NOT zeroized would not be detected while the row count stays in range. The .contains() anchors are also satisfiable by any occurrence of the needle elsewhere in the file, not bound to the row's symbol.
evidence: I read the cited region: lint_zeroize_discipline.rs:261-266 asserts only `(18..=35).contains(&n)` (the comment at :257-259 itself calls it a 'Loose bound'). The evidence loop at :271-287 does `source.contains(needle)` over the whole-file string read at :273 — a file-scope substring, hit-tested at :280, not anchored to the row's symbol/line. The module doc (:12-16) frames it as 'the implementing source file contains a stable evidence anchor' = presence, not coverage. By contrast lint_argv_secret_flags.rs:184-223 has true set-equality closures (flag_axis_set_equals_gui_schema etc.) against the live gui-schema surface.
suggested_fix: Where feasible, add a closure enumerating the actual zeroize-relevant surface (build-time scan for secret-typed buffers / Zeroizing wrappers) and set-equal it against the canon, mirroring the argv lint's *_set_equals_gui_schema pattern. At minimum narrow the count range and bind each evidence anchor to the row's symbol rather than a file-level substring.
verification_note: Confirmed at source: count gate is the literal `(18..=35).contains(&n)` at :262 (17-wide), and the evidence check is whole-file `source.contains(needle)` at :280 with no symbol/line binding. Confirmed the argv lint genuinely DOES have the missing set-equality closures (lint_argv_secret_flags.rs:184-223) so the contrast the finding draws is accurate. Observation is correct — this is a robustness gap in the lint's completeness guarantee, no demonstrated live un-zeroized secret.

title: Several in-crate synthesize.rs tests assert prefix/non-emptiness (presence) rather than value (correctness)
claim: The in-crate synthesize sanity tests assert ms1.starts_with("ms1"), mk1/md1 all start_with their HRP, and non-emptiness. A regression emitting a syntactically-valid but WRONG card (wrong key/network/account) would still carry the right HRP and pass. The byte-determinism tests run the same input twice and compare — they catch only nondeterminism, not wrong values. The gap is contained because value-correctness is pinned elsewhere.
evidence: Read source: synthesize.rs:973-978 (full_bundle_emits_three_cards) and :988-991 (watch_only) only check HRP prefix + non-emptiness; mk1_chunk_set_id_is_deterministic_across_runs (:995-1022) calls synthesize_full twice with identical args and asserts a.mk1==b.mk1 / a.md1==b.md1 / a.ms1==b.ms1 — tautological for value-correctness. However correctness IS covered: cross_binding_holds_round_trip (:1024-1050) decodes mk1+md1 and asserts decoded_mk1.policy_id_stubs[0]==policy_id[..4], decoded_mk1.xpub==xpub, origin_fingerprint==Some(fp); multisig_full_*_byte_identical (:1052-1083) decodes and checks stub count + is_wallet_policy; plus integration goldens (cli_restore_multisig / cli_build_descriptor).
suggested_fix: No action required given the cross_binding round-trip cell and integration goldens cover value-correctness. If the prefix cells are ever relied on as the primary oracle, upgrade them to a single byte-exact committed golden per shape.
verification_note: Confirmed the prefix/non-empty cells at :973-978/:988-991 and the same-input determinism compare at :1015-1021 are not value oracles. Also independently confirmed the finding's own containment claim by reading cross_binding_holds_round_trip (:1037-1049): it decodes and value-checks policy_id_stubs/xpub/fingerprint, so a real value oracle exists in-crate. Observation with no forced action is the correct disposition — the finding is essentially a note-to-future-editors, not a live false-green.

title: gui_schema_conditional_drift gate runs against any `mnemonic` on PATH; its module doc falsely claims it skips when MNEMONIC_BIN is unset
claim: The module doc (:13) states the gate is 'Skipped (returns early) when MNEMONIC_BIN is unset', but mnemonic_bin() always returns Some (PATH fallback to "mnemonic"), so a stale/unpinned `mnemonic` on PATH makes the gate run against the WRONG binary and pass misleadingly. It truly skips only when no `mnemonic` resolves at all.
evidence: Verified at source: mnemonic_bin() (:28-33) does `std::env::var("MNEMONIC_BIN").ok().map(PathBuf::from).or_else(|| Some(PathBuf::from("mnemonic")))` — never None. The `let Some(bin) = mnemonic_bin() else { ...return }` at :194 therefore never takes the skip arm on unset. :198-210 then does a best-effort `mnemonic --help` PATH probe and runs the gate if it succeeds. Doc :13 directly contradicts this. Same PATH-fallback shape (without the misleading doc) in canonicity_drift.rs:32-44 and tree_form.rs:56-64.
suggested_fix: Correct the module doc to describe real behavior (runs against MNEMONIC_BIN, else any `mnemonic` on PATH; only skips when none resolves). Stronger: require MNEMONIC_BIN explicitly (eprintln+skip when unset) rather than silently falling back to PATH, and/or log the resolved binary path + its gui-schema version so a stale-binary pass is visible.
verification_note: Confirmed the doc lie and the never-None fallback chain by reading the cited lines. Downgraded from important to minor: CI is correct (schema-mirror.yml cargo-test-full-suite sets MNEMONIC_BIN=mnemonic with the freshly cargo-installed pinned binary, ~lines 122-127), so the only impact is a dev-laptop false-green plus a misleading doc — a real but low-blast-radius hygiene/robustness issue, not a CI-gate failure.

title: The HIGH-severity secret-drift gate silently skips when gui-schema version < 5 (or binary absent); CI smoke only asserts version>=1
claim: fetch_v5_schema() returns None (→ silent PASS) when the binary can't spawn, exits non-zero, JSON won't parse, OR version<5. The version<5 branch means a regressed/pre-v5 pinned binary would silently disable the secret-misclassification gate (the gate guarding the v0.3.0..v0.3.2 BIP-39 persistence-leak class), and the CI smoke step only asserts version>=1, so nothing else catches it. Latent today (toolkit emits v5), not live.
evidence: Verified: schema_mirror_secret_drift.rs:73-80 returns None on `root.version < 5`; :86-91 `let Some(...) = fetch_v5_schema() else { return; }` turns None into a silent skip. CI smoke at schema-mirror.yml:94 asserts only `d['version']>=1 and d['cli']=='mnemonic'`. Toolkit currently emits `version: 5` at crates/mnemonic-toolkit/src/cmd/gui_schema.rs:1054 (verified) — so the version<5 skip is latent, not currently triggered.
suggested_fix: Add a CI smoke assertion that gui-schema version >= 5 (the version the secret-drift gate requires), and/or panic-instead-of-skip when MNEMONIC_BIN is explicitly set (the CI case) so a wrong-version pinned binary fails loud. Keep the silent skip only for the genuinely-no-binary dev case.
verification_note: Independently confirmed the four None-returning conditions and the silent-skip at the call site, the CI smoke asserting only version>=1, and that the toolkit currently emits version 5. Severity minor as the auditor rated it: the vacuous-skip is a poor failure mode on a high-value gate but is not currently exploitable (v5 is live).

title: Paste-warn: only the should_warn_on_paste predicate is tested; there is in fact NO live paste-warn modal wiring at all — should_warn_on_paste has zero production callers
claim: widget_secret.rs asserts only the pure predicate should_warn_on_paste(flag,len) + SecretLineEdit buffer/zeroize transitions; its own doc (:18-24) defers the live check (paste into the rendered secret widget sets the app's pending_paste_warn modal) as 'out of B.1 scope', so the render-event→modal wiring is uncovered.
evidence: Verified: widget_secret.rs:18-24 defers the app-state assertion; :42-71 exercises only should_warn_on_paste + SecretLineEdit. STRONGER than the finding states: grepping src/ shows `should_warn_on_paste` is DEFINED at src/secrets.rs:194 and has ZERO callers anywhere in src/ — only test references (tests/secrets.rs, tests/widget_secret.rs). There is no `pending_paste_warn` field in src/ at all; the only live modal in main.rs (:83, :832) is the RUN-CONFIRM modal, not a paste-warn modal. So there is no live paste-warn wiring to regress — the affordance is unwired dead-but-tested code.
suggested_fix: Either wire should_warn_on_paste into the secret-widget render path and add a kittest driving a >=threshold paste into a rendered secret widget that asserts the warn-modal intent fires, or remove the unwired predicate/modal text and the tests that pin it. File a FOLLOWUP recording the decision (wire vs. remove) since the run-confirm modal already provides a pre-spawn argv-confirmation backstop.
verification_note: Independently confirmed via grep that should_warn_on_paste (src/secrets.rs:194) is never called outside tests and that no paste-warn modal state exists in src/ — only a run-confirm modal. This makes the auditor's 'live wiring untested' correct and arguably understated, but severity stays minor: the run-confirm modal still gates the spawn, so no funds/secret-leak path is opened by the missing paste-warn affordance.

title: conditional_visibility.rs cells assert the conditional fn's output map, not that the live render loop hides/disables accordingly — mitigated by the assembler-side emit gate
claim: Every cell calls run_conditional(name,state) → the pure conditional fn → and asserts the returned (flag,Visibility) map; none drives the live form to confirm the rendered widget is actually hidden/disabled. The live render application of Disabled/Hidden (the main.rs render loop) has no kittest. Mitigated because argv_assembler_visibility.rs proves emit-time suppression, so a Hidden flag's value cannot leak to argv even if the render loop mis-rendered.
evidence: Verified: conditional_visibility.rs:36-40 run_conditional just invokes sub.conditional(state); cells assert vis_of(...) on that output. The live render loop DOES apply the map — main.rs:487-489 `if matches!(v, Hidden) { continue; }` and main.rs:513-515 `ui.add_enabled_ui(!matches!(v, Disabled), ...)` — but no kittest asserts a Disabled flag renders non-interactive. Emit-time suppression is independently covered by argv_assembler_visibility.rs (verified: e.g. bundle_threshold_suppressed_when_single_sig_template asserts the assembler drops suppressed flags from argv).
suggested_fix: Optionally add a few kittest cells that render a subcommand form via the main render loop (or a thin lib seam over it) and assert a Disabled flag's widget is non-interactive and a Hidden flag's widget is absent. Low priority given the assembler-side emit gate already prevents a suppressed value from reaching argv.
verification_note: Confirmed the cells are pure-conditional-fn assertions and that the live render-loop application (main.rs Hidden-continue + add_enabled_ui(!Disabled)) is untested, while the assembler-side emit gate (argv_assembler_visibility.rs) does exist. Observation severity is correct: the auditor explicitly notes no such bug exists today and the funds-relevant emit path is gated independently.

title: validate_tap_script_tree only checks the leaf-root tag, never recurses into compound leaf bodies
claim: The tap-script-tree leaf validator descends only through Tag::TapTree internal nodes; for any non-TapTree node it treats the node as a leaf and checks ONLY that node's own root tag against is_forbidden_leaf_tag. A compound leaf such as and_v(v:pk(@0), wsh(...)) has only its root tag (AndV, not forbidden) inspected, so a forbidden wrapper tag nested inside a tap leaf is not caught structurally by this validator. The doc-comment at validate.rs:140 ('Validate that all leaves in a tap-script-tree are permitted-leaf tags per §6.3.1') overstates what the function actually enforces.
evidence: walk_tap_tree_leaves (validate.rs:145-162) recurses only when matches!(node.tag, Tag::TapTree) and iterates Body::Children; the else branch (validate.rs:153-161) calls is_forbidden_leaf_tag(node.tag) on the single node and returns Ok for any compound operator. It never walks the Body::Children of AndV/AndOr/OrD/Thresh etc. The forbidden set (validate.rs:164-168) is {Wpkh,Tr,Wsh,Sh,Pkh,Multi,SortedMulti}. The tests only exercise a bare Wsh leaf (validate.rs:317-326) and a bare PkK leaf (validate.rs:328-335) — no nested-forbidden-in-compound-leaf case.
suggested_fix: Either recurse walk_tap_tree_leaves into Body::Children / Body::Variable / Body::Tr child bodies so nested forbidden wrapper tags are rejected structurally, or narrow the doc-comment to state the check is root-tag-only and that full leaf-validity is delegated to the miniscript type-checker.
verification_note: Confirmed at validate.rs:145-169: walk_tap_tree_leaves only recurses on Tag::TapTree via Body::Children; the else branch tests only node.tag, never descending into Body::Children/Variable of compound leaves. Confirmed the safety net the auditor cited IS real: node_to_miniscript<Tap> at to_miniscript.rs:448-453 returns a typed AddressDerivationFailed for (Tag::Tr|Wsh|Sh|Wpkh|Pkh, _) appearing inside a miniscript context, and tree_to_taptree (to_miniscript.rs:277) routes single/compound tap leaves through node_to_miniscript<Tap>. So a forbidden wrapper nested inside a compound tap leaf is rejected with a typed error at derivation time, not silently mis-derived. No funds/wrong-output impact; correctly minor (doc/structural shallowness behind a sound type-checker fence).

title: multi_a / sortedmulti_a key count capped at 32 by the 5-bit n-1 wire field (tapscript runtime ceiling is 999)
claim: The multi-family wire layout encodes (k-1) and (n-1) in 5 bits each, so both k and n are limited to 1..=32. On encode, write_node validates (1..=32).contains for k and indices.len() and rejects larger with ThresholdOutOfRange/ChildCountOutOfRange; on decode, k and count are read_bits(5)+1 (max 32). Tapscript multi_a/sortedmulti_a permit far larger thresholds (rust-miniscript MAX_PUBKEYS_IN_CHECKSIGADD = 999), so a legitimate large taproot multisig (e.g. 40-of-67) cannot be engraved as md1.
evidence: tree.rs:108-115 enforce (1..=32) for both *k and indices.len(); tree.rs:227-228 read k=read_bits(5)+1 and count=read_bits(5)+1 (each max 32). MAX_PUBKEYS_IN_CHECKSIGADD = 999 confirmed in miniscript-13.0.0/src/miniscript/limits.rs:38; md-codec pins miniscript 13.0.0 (descriptor-mnemonic/Cargo.toml:18). Note multi/sortedmulti (legacy/segwit) is bounded by MAX_PUBKEYS_PER_MULTISIG=20 (<32) so the cap only truncates the multi_a/sortedmulti_a family.
suggested_fix: No action required if 32 is an accepted product limit; if larger taproot multisigs are in scope this is a wire-format-break design item. Either way, document the 32-key ceiling explicitly in the spec/manual so it is a known limitation rather than a surprise refusal.
verification_note: Confirmed the 5-bit encode/decode fields at tree.rs:95-121 (encode) and tree.rs:226-237 (decode) cap both k and n at 32. Independently read miniscript-13.0.0/src/miniscript/limits.rs:38 — MAX_PUBKEYS_IN_CHECKSIGADD=999 — confirming the 999 runtime ceiling. No correctness/funds bug (oversized templates are cleanly refused with a typed error on encode); accurate observation about a wire-format design limit.

title: SortedMultiA round-trips on the wire but fails address derivation — correctly fenced, worth tracking as a partial-capability shape
claim: A tr(sortedmulti_a(...)) md1 string encodes (tag 0x09), decodes back to the AST cleanly and passes decoder validation, but to_miniscript_descriptor cannot build an address because the pinned rust-miniscript v13 has no Terminal::SortedMultiA. The converter returns a typed AddressDerivationFailed — fenced gracefully (no panic, no silent wrong address).
evidence: to_miniscript.rs:406-411 returns failed('Tag::SortedMultiA must be a tap-leaf root child; rust-miniscript v13 has no Terminal::SortedMultiA fragment'). tag.rs:109/173 confirm SortedMultiA <-> 0x09 round-trips on the wire; tree.rs:226-237 decodes it into Body::MultiKeys cleanly. tree_to_taptree (to_miniscript.rs:262-279) only special-cases Tag::TapTree and otherwise falls through to node_to_miniscript<Tap>, so even a bare single-leaf tr(sortedmulti_a) hits the rejection. miniscript pinned at 13.0.0 (Cargo.toml:18).
suggested_fix: None needed for correctness — the fence is sound. Keep the md-codec-sortedmulti-a-to-miniscript-rendering-gap FOLLOWUP open and ensure any md-cli address/derive surface for this shape surfaces the typed error rather than appearing to succeed.
verification_note: Confirmed the typed-error fence at to_miniscript.rs:406-411 (AddressDerivationFailed, no panic), the wire round-trip via tag.rs:109/173 + tree.rs:226-237, and the tree_to_taptree fall-through at to_miniscript.rs:277. title: TLV decoder's <=7-bit rollback-as-padding tolerance is wider than the wire layer's actual padding (<=4 bits single-string, 0 chunked)
claim: On any TLV parse failure the decoder rolls back and treats the trailing bits as padding when remaining_at_entry_start <= 7. The codex32 single-string path only ever leaves <=4 bits of symbol-padding, and the chunk path decodes a byte-aligned payload (0 padding). The 7-bit window is therefore wider than any padding the encoder produces, so a malformed wire whose final 5-7 trailing bits happen to fail a TLV parse is accepted as a clean decode rather than rejected.
evidence: tlv.rs:296 'if remaining_at_entry_start <= 7 { break; }' on the parse-failure rollback branch (tlv.rs:286-302). Single-string decode uses symbol_aligned_bit_count = 5*data_symbols.len() (codex32.rs:128; decode.rs:80-81), with <=4 padding bits (codex32 comment at :149). Chunked decode_payload is called with full_bytes.len()*8 (chunk.rs:376), byte-aligned, 0 padding. The TLV read loop (tlv.rs:215-221) only enters the parse attempt when >=5 bits remain, so the 5-7 bit slack window above the real <=4 boundary is reachable only by hand-crafted wires the encoder never emits.
suggested_fix: Tighten the tolerance to the actual maximum padding the wire layer produces (<=4 bits for the symbol-aligned single-string path), or document why 7 was chosen; the wider window trades a small amount of malformed-wire detection for headroom the encoder never exercises.
verification_note: Confirmed tlv.rs:296 uses <=7 on the parse-failure rollback path. Confirmed single-string padding is <=4 (codex32.rs:128 bit_count=5*data_symbols.len(); decode.rs:80-81 threads symbol_aligned_bit_count) and chunked path is byte-aligned 0 padding (chunk.rs:376 full_bytes.len()*8). The slack only affects a hand-crafted malformed wire with exactly 5-7 trailing junk bits; the decoded descriptor is still structurally complete and valid (header/tree/validators already passed), so there is no funds or wrong-output impact — only slightly weaker malformed-tail detection. Correctly observation.

title: combine_shares skips entropy-length validation that decode enforces → ms combine --to phrase panics on non-standard-length share sets
location: crates/ms-codec/src/envelope.rs:167-188 (dispatch_payload Entr arm, no validate); crates/ms-codec/src/shares.rs:236-242 (combine_shares); crates/ms-cli/src/cmd/combine.rs:96-97 (emit_phrase .expect panic)
claim: combine_shares recovers an Entr payload via dispatch_payload, whose Entr arm (envelope.rs:169-172) returns Payload::Entr(data[1..].to_vec()) WITHOUT calling .validate() (only the Mnem arm validates, line 180). Unlike the single-string decode() path (decode.rs rules 9 pre/post-dispatch + rule 10 p.validate() at line 85), combine_shares applies no payload-length gate. codex32 from_string accepts any string in [48,94) with a valid checksum and interpolate_at only enforces equal intra-set length — so a valid-checksum share set at a non-ms length (e.g. total 53) recovers an Entr payload whose data[1..] length is not in {16,20,24,28,32}. combine.rs:97 then calls Mnemonic::from_entropy_in(...).expect("...cannot fail"), which for a non-standard bit-count returns Err(BadEntropyBitCount) → .expect() PANICS (abort, exit 101) on the DEFAULT --to phrase. --to entropy silently hex-dumps the wrong-length bytes; only --to ms1 errors cleanly (encode re-validates).
evidence: envelope.rs:169-172 Entr arm: `RESERVED_PREFIX => { Payload::Entr(data[1..].to_vec()) }` — no validate, contrast Mnem arm line 180 `p.validate()?`. shares.rs:240-242: `let data = Zeroizing::new(secret.parts().data()); let payload = dispatch_payload(&data)?; Ok((Tag::ENTR, payload))`. combine.rs:96-97: `Mnemonic::from_entropy_in(lang, entropy).expect("combine_shares validates entropy length; from_entropy_in cannot fail")` — message asserts a guarantee combine_shares does NOT provide. codex32-0.1.0 lib.rs:148-155 from_string accepts s.len() in [48,94) for any valid short checksum; lib.rs:236-254 interpolate_at only checks shares[0].len()==share.len(), not ms validity. bip39-2.2.2 lib.rs:207-220 from_entropy_in returns Err(BadEntropyBitCount) when nb_bits%32!=0 or out of [128,256]. No CLI-side length gate (grep of combine.rs / main.rs dispatch shows none). Payload::validate (payload.rs:64-90) WOULD catch this via Error::PayloadLengthMismatch but is never called on the Entr combine path. negative.rs rule_10 documents the single-string path is protected by rule 9 firing first — combine has no equivalent.
suggested_fix: Add payload.validate()? to the Entr arm of dispatch_payload (envelope.rs:169-172), so both combine_shares and any other dispatch_payload caller surface a clean Error::PayloadLengthMismatch instead of panicking downstream. Add a regression test feeding combine_shares a valid-checksum share set whose recovered Entr payload length is not in VALID_ENTR_LENGTHS and assert Err, not panic (and verify --to entropy no longer emits wrong-length bytes).
verification_note: Personally re-grounded the entire chain against source: (1) envelope.rs:167-188 — confirmed the Entr arm has NO .validate() call while the Mnem arm (line 180) does; (2) decode.rs:42-90 — confirmed the single-string path IS protected (rule 9 length gate pre/post-dispatch + rule 10 p.validate() at line 85), so combine is the genuine gap; (3) codex32-0.1.0 lib.rs:148-155 (from_string accepts [48,94)) and lib.rs:236-254 (interpolate_at only enforces equal intra-set string length, NOT ms validity) + Parts::data() at lib.rs:399 (byte len = floor(payload_chars*5/8)) — confirmed a non-ms-length valid-checksum set produces a non-standard entropy length; (4) bip39-2.2.2 lib.rs:215-219 — confirmed from_entropy_in returns Err(BadEntropyBitCount) for bit-counts not %32==0 or outside [128,256], so .expect() at combine.rs:97 panics; (5) confirmed no pre-combine length gate exists in combine.rs or main.rs dispatch. Severity is correctly important (ungraceful panic / contract-violation on a real input path; not funds loss — the output bytes ARE the recovered secret, just non-standard length), so I did NOT escalate to critical.

title: combine_shares recovered secret-at-S Codex32String holds plaintext entropy with no scrub-on-drop
claim: combine_shares binds `let secret = Codex32String::interpolate_at(...)` (shares.rs:236); Codex32String is a newtype over String (codex32-0.1.0 lib.rs:102 `pub struct Codex32String(String)`) with no Drop/Zeroize, so the recovered secret-at-S plaintext lives in that String buffer un-scrubbed until the allocator reuses it. Only the derived `data` Vec (line 240) is Zeroizing-wrapped. Optionally widen the rust-codex32-zeroize-upstream FOLLOWUP to explicitly cover the combine_shares interpolate_at recovery binding so future readers know both encode and decode sides carry the gap.
verification_note: Confirmed at source: shares.rs:236 binds `secret` as a Codex32String and only the line-240 `data` Vec is Zeroizing-wrapped; codex32-0.1.0 lib.rs:102 is `pub struct Codex32String(String)` with no Drop/Zeroize (grep found only Display + inherent impls); FOLLOWUPS.md:129 scopes rust-codex32-zeroize-upstream to envelope::package's from_seed buffer only; lint_zeroize_discipline.rs:67 anchors the `data` Vec literal, not the secret binding.
